enrichment.short_description: CasaOS is a free, open-source personal cloud operating system for self-hosting apps and files on devices like Raspberry Pi and ZimaBoard.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CasaOS: free, open-source personal cloud OS for self-hosting files and Docker apps on Raspberry Pi, ZimaBoard, and NUC hardware.

features.title: One-click app store
features.description: 20+ pre-installed Docker-based apps plus 50+ community-verified apps available for one-click installation.

features.title: Custom Docker app support
features.description: Import and run existing Docker applications alongside apps from the CasaOS store.

features.title: Centralized file storage
features.description: Store files, photos, and videos on your own hardware with cross-device syncing.

features.title: Remote access
features.description: Access files and applications outside the local network with secure connections.

features.title: Multi-hardware support
features.description: Runs on Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC, ZimaBoard, and other amd64/armv7/arm64 devices.

pros: Fully open source under Apache 2.0, with 23.9k GitHub stars
No subscription fees
Runs on affordable, low-power hardware like Raspberry Pi and ZimaBoard
Growing curated app ecosystem
Active Discord and GitHub community
cons: Requires owning and setting up your own hardware
Community-maintained app store quality can vary by app
No official vendor-backed enterprise support tier mentioned on the site

faqs.question: Is CasaOS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CasaOS is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no subscription fees.

faqs.question: What hardware does CasaOS support?
faqs.answer: Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC, ZimaBoard, and other amd64/armv7/arm64 devices running Ubuntu, Debian, Raspberry Pi OS, or CentOS.

faqs.question: Who maintains CasaOS?
faqs.answer: CasaOS is developed and maintained by IceWhale Tech, operating under ZimaSpace.

faqs.question: Can I install my own Docker apps on CasaOS?
faqs.answer: Yes, CasaOS supports importing existing Docker applications in addition to its built-in app store.

use_cases.title: Home NAS replacement
use_cases.description: Store and sync files, photos, and videos on your own hardware instead of paying for cloud storage.
use_cases.audience: Home users wanting to own their data

use_cases.title: Self-hosted app hub
use_cases.description: Run a curated set of Docker apps from a single dashboard on low-power hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is CasaOS?
paragraphs: CasaOS is an open-source personal cloud operating system that turns hardware like a Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC, or ZimaBoard into a self-hosted server for files, media, and apps.

It ships with a one-click app store of 20+ pre-installed Docker apps and 50+ community-verified apps, and it also supports importing existing Docker applications, making it a flexible base for a home server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CasaOS is for
paragraphs: CasaOS suits homelab enthusiasts and privacy-conscious users who want to run their own file storage and apps on inexpensive hardware instead of paying for commercial cloud subscriptions.

enrichment.short_description: Umbrel is a personal home server platform (umbrelOS) with a one-click app store for self-hosting files, media, AI models, and a Bitcoin node.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Umbrel: personal home cloud platform with a one-click app store for self-hosted files, AI models, Bitcoin nodes, and media, from $549.

features.title: One-click app store
features.description: 30+ pre-installed apps including Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Plex, Tailscale, and n8n.

features.title: Local AI hosting
features.description: Run AI models such as DeepSeek R1 and Llama 3, plus an OpenClaw agent, directly on the device.

features.title: Bitcoin and Lightning node
features.description: Run a full Bitcoin node with integrated wallet support.

features.title: Media streaming
features.description: Stream movies and TV shows to multiple devices from your own server.

features.title: Network-wide ad blocking
features.description: Built-in Pi-hole integration blocks ads across the whole network.

features.title: Home automation
features.description: Home Assistant integration supports 1,000+ smart-home devices.

pros: Turnkey Umbrel Home/Pro hardware options for non-technical users
Curated one-click app store with 30+ apps
Supports running AI models locally
Integrates a Bitcoin/Lightning node and home automation out of the box
umbrelOS is free to install on your own hardware
cons: Dedicated Umbrel Home/Pro hardware carries a significant upfront cost ($549-$699+)
Local AI model hosting requires sufficiently capable hardware
Limited to apps available in the Umbrel App Store

faqs.question: Is umbrelOS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, umbrelOS is free to install on your own hardware.

faqs.question: Does Umbrel sell dedicated hardware?
faqs.answer: Yes, Umbrel Home starts at $549 and Umbrel Pro starts at $699, with financing options such as $174.75/month for 4 months.

faqs.question: What apps are available on Umbrel?
faqs.answer: 30+ apps including Nextcloud, Jellyfin, Plex, Tailscale, and n8n.

faqs.question: Can Umbrel run AI models?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports running local AI models such as DeepSeek R1 and Llama 3.

use_cases.title: Personal cloud storage
use_cases.description: Replace paid cloud storage services with self-hosted file, photo, and video storage.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted AI
use_cases.description: Run open AI models locally without sending data to third-party cloud services.

use_cases.title: Home Bitcoin node
use_cases.description: Run a full Bitcoin and Lightning node with wallet support from home.

features: Free operating system for self-hosting on your own hardware

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Umbrel?
paragraphs: Umbrel is a personal home server platform built around umbrelOS, its free operating system for self-hosting files, media, AI models, and a Bitcoin node.
Umbrel also sells dedicated Umbrel Home and Umbrel Pro hardware, starting at $549 and $699 respectively, alongside its 30+ app one-click store.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Umbrel is for
paragraphs: Umbrel targets users who want to own their data and run their own cloud, AI, and Bitcoin infrastructure at home, whether via purchased hardware or by installing umbrelOS on their own device.

enrichment.short_description: OpenNebula is an open-source cloud and virtualization management platform for building private, hybrid, and edge clouds as an alternative to VMware.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenNebula: open-source cloud and virtualization management platform for private, hybrid, and edge clouds, maintained by OpenNebula Systems.

features.title: DRS-style scheduling and HA
features.description: Automated resource scheduling and high availability for hosted workloads.

features.title: Multi-site federation
features.description: Federate multiple sites into a single managed cloud.

features.title: Kubernetes integration
features.description: Orchestrate Kubernetes clusters alongside virtualized workloads.

features.title: GPU and accelerated computing
features.description: Supports GPU workloads through an NVIDIA partnership.

features.title: Enterprise storage integrations
features.description: Connects with NetApp, Pure Storage, and Veeam.

features.title: Secure multi-tenancy
features.description: Isolates tenants for shared infrastructure environments.

pros: Source code publicly available on GitHub
Avoids proprietary hypervisor licensing costs (positioned as a VMware alternative)
Supports GPU and multi-hypervisor environments
Multi-site federation for distributed deployments
Optional integrated Ubuntu Pro, RHEL, or SUSE subscriptions
cons: Enterprise Subscription pricing is not published on the site (quote-based)
Site content is licensed CC BY-NC-SA (non-commercial), separate from the platform's own source license
Requires infrastructure expertise to self-host and operate

faqs.question: Is OpenNebula free?
faqs.answer: A Community version is available at no cost; paid Enterprise Subscriptions with additional services also exist.

faqs.question: What does OpenNebula replace?
faqs.answer: It is positioned as an alternative to proprietary cloud/virtualization platforms like VMware.

faqs.question: Does OpenNebula support GPU workloads?
faqs.answer: Yes, including support enabled through an NVIDIA partnership.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenNebula?
faqs.answer: OpenNebula Systems owns and maintains the platform.

use_cases.title: Private and sovereign cloud
use_cases.description: Build a self-managed private cloud without relying on public cloud providers.

use_cases.title: Edge computing
use_cases.description: Deploy and manage workloads across distributed edge locations.

use_cases.title: VMware migration
use_cases.description: Migrate virtualization workloads off proprietary platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenNebula?
paragraphs: OpenNebula is a cloud and virtualization management platform used to build private, hybrid, and edge clouds, with support for GPU workloads, Kubernetes orchestration, and multi-site federation.
It is maintained by OpenNebula Systems and offers both a Community edition and paid Enterprise Subscriptions, along with integrated Ubuntu Pro, RHEL, or SUSE subscription options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenNebula is for
paragraphs: OpenNebula suits organizations that want to run their own private or edge cloud infrastructure rather than paying for proprietary virtualization licenses or public cloud services.

enrichment.short_description: OpenStack is a free, Apache 2.0-licensed open-source platform for managing large pools of compute, storage, and networking across clouds.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenStack: free, open-source Apache 2.0 platform for managing compute, storage, and networking at scale, governed by the OpenInfra Foundation.

features.title: Compute, bare-metal, and container management
features.description: Manage virtual machines, bare metal, and containers through a common set of services.

features.title: Orchestration and fault management
features.description: Automates orchestration, fault handling, and service management for cloud infrastructure.

features.title: High availability
features.description: Provides HA support for applications running on OpenStack.

features.title: API and dashboard control
features.description: Resources can be managed via APIs or a web dashboard.

features.title: Multi-deployment support
features.description: Supports on-premises, public cloud, and edge computing deployments.

pros: Fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no licensing fees
Governed by the vendor-neutral OpenInfra Foundation
Proven at large scale (Walmart runs 170,000+ cores; China Mobile runs 50,000+ servers)
Powers 180+ public cloud data centers
Manages 40+ million cores globally across the OpenStack community
cons: No official managed or paid tier from the project itself
Commercial support must be sourced from third-party distributions
Complex to deploy and operate at scale

faqs.question: Is OpenStack free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenStack is open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who governs OpenStack?
faqs.answer: It is a top-level project of the OpenInfra Foundation.

faqs.question: How widely is OpenStack used?
faqs.answer: It powers 180+ public cloud data centers and manages 40+ million cores globally, per the OpenStack Foundation.

faqs.question: What can OpenStack manage?
faqs.answer: Virtual machines, bare metal, and containers, along with orchestration and fault management.

use_cases.title: Public cloud infrastructure
use_cases.description: Operate large-scale public cloud data centers.

use_cases.title: Private enterprise cloud
use_cases.description: Run internal compute, storage, and networking infrastructure at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenStack?
paragraphs: OpenStack is an open-source cloud infrastructure platform that provides common services for managing large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources through APIs or a dashboard.

It is licensed under Apache 2.0, developed by the open-source community, and governed by the OpenInfra Foundation. It powers 180+ public cloud data centers and is used by organizations including Walmart and China Mobile.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenStack is for
paragraphs: OpenStack is aimed at cloud providers, telecoms, and large enterprises that need to operate their own compute, storage, and networking infrastructure at scale without relying on a single vendor.

enrichment.short_description: Harvester is a free, open-source hyperconverged infrastructure platform for running VMs and cloud-native workloads on bare metal, built by SUSE/Rancher.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harvester: free, open-source hyperconverged infrastructure for running VMs and cloud-native workloads on bare metal, from SUSE/Rancher.

features.title: Unified VM and container management
features.description: A single pane of glass for both virtualization and cloud-native workload management.

features.title: Kubernetes-in-VM support
features.description: Runs Kubernetes inside VMs alongside containerized workloads on the same bare-metal cluster.

features.title: Built on proven open-source components
features.description: Uses Linux, KVM, Kubernetes, KubeVirt, and Longhorn instead of proprietary technology.

features.title: Rancher ecosystem integration
features.description: Works alongside Rancher, Elemental, and Fleet.

pros: 100% open source and free with no licensing fees
Built entirely on established open-source components (KVM, KubeVirt, Longhorn)
Maintained by SUSE/Rancher
Suited for both datacenter and edge deployments
cons: Requires bare-metal hardware and infrastructure expertise to deploy
Smaller ecosystem than mature commercial hypervisors like VMware
Enterprise support terms are not detailed on the homepage

faqs.question: Is Harvester free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is 100% open source and free to download and deploy with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who maintains Harvester?
faqs.answer: SUSE/Rancher maintains Harvester as part of its broader Rancher ecosystem.

faqs.question: What does Harvester run on?
faqs.answer: Bare-metal servers, combining virtualization and cloud-native workload management.

faqs.question: What technologies is Harvester built on?
faqs.answer: Linux, KVM, Kubernetes, KubeVirt, and Longhorn.

use_cases.title: Modernizing VM workloads
use_cases.description: Run existing VMs alongside new containerized applications on the same infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Edge computing
use_cases.description: Deploy hyperconverged infrastructure at edge locations without proprietary licensing costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Harvester?
paragraphs: Harvester is an open-source hyperconverged infrastructure (HCI) solution that manages both virtualization and cloud-native workloads on bare-metal servers from a single pane of glass.
It is built on mature open-source components including Linux, KVM, Kubernetes, KubeVirt, and Longhorn, and is maintained by SUSE/Rancher as part of the broader Rancher ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Harvester is for
paragraphs: Harvester is aimed at infrastructure teams that want to run VM-based and cloud-native workloads on the same bare-metal hardware without paying for proprietary HCI licensing.

enrichment.short_description: KubeVirt is an open-source, CNCF-hosted project that adds a virtualization API to Kubernetes, letting teams run VMs alongside containers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KubeVirt: free, open-source CNCF project adding a virtualization API to Kubernetes to run VMs alongside containerized workloads.

features.title: Virtualization API for Kubernetes
features.description: Manage virtual machines using native Kubernetes APIs and tooling.

features.title: Run VMs alongside containers
features.description: Combine containerized applications and VM-based workloads on the same cluster.

features.title: Gradual modernization support
features.description: Decompose virtualized workloads into microservices over time instead of an all-at-once migration.

features.title: Multiple quickstart paths
features.description: Getting-started guides for MiniKube, Kind, Killercoda, and cloud providers.

pros: Free and open source
CNCF-hosted incubating project with community governance
Lets teams keep hard-to-containerize VM workloads on Kubernetes
Multiple quickstart options for testing before production use
cons: Listed as an incubating (not yet graduated) CNCF project
Requires an existing Kubernetes cluster to run
Aimed primarily at infrastructure/platform teams rather than end users

faqs.question: Is KubeVirt free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is an open-source project with no license fees.

faqs.question: Who governs KubeVirt?
faqs.answer: It is hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) as an incubating project.

faqs.question: What problem does KubeVirt solve?
faqs.answer: It lets teams run VM-based workloads that can't easily be containerized alongside containers on Kubernetes.

faqs.question: How do I try KubeVirt?
faqs.answer: The project provides quickstarts for MiniKube, Kind, Killercoda, and cloud providers.

use_cases.title: Running legacy VMs on Kubernetes
use_cases.description: Keep VM-based applications running on the same platform as new containerized services.

use_cases.title: Gradual application modernization
use_cases.description: Decompose virtualized workloads into microservices incrementally.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is KubeVirt?
paragraphs: KubeVirt is an open-source project that provides a virtualization API for Kubernetes, letting organizations manage both containerized applications and virtual machines within a unified platform.
It is hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) as an incubating project and is aimed at teams with existing VM-based workloads that cannot be easily containerized.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who KubeVirt is for
paragraphs: KubeVirt suits organizations adopting Kubernetes that still need to run VM-based infrastructure, or teams gradually modernizing legacy virtualized applications into microservices.

enrichment.short_description: Nomad is HashiCorp's scheduler and orchestrator for deploying containers and non-containerized applications across cloud and on-prem environments.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nomad by HashiCorp: scheduler and orchestrator for containers, binaries, and batch jobs across cloud and on-prem infrastructure.

features.title: Hybrid job scheduling
features.description: Orchestrate, deploy, and manage containers, binaries, and batch jobs across on-prem and cloud environments.

features.title: Non-containerized workload support
features.description: Runs containerized and non-containerized applications from the same scheduler.

features.title: Task driver extensibility
features.description: Supports multiple task drivers for different workload types.

features.title: Advanced scheduling
features.description: Includes affinities and preemption for fine-grained placement control.

features.title: HashiCorp ecosystem integration
features.description: Integrates with Consul for service discovery and Vault for secrets management.

features.title: Enterprise governance
features.description: Enterprise edition adds access controls and governance policies.

pros: Works with both containers and non-containerized applications
Integrates natively with Consul and Vault
Flexible, extensible task-driver model
Supports hybrid on-prem and cloud deployment
cons: Enterprise tier pricing is not publicly listed (quote-based)
Advanced governance features are gated behind the Enterprise edition
Smaller ecosystem and community than Kubernetes

faqs.question: Is Nomad free?
faqs.answer: An open-source/Community edition is available; HashiCorp also offers a paid Enterprise edition with additional governance features.

faqs.question: What does Nomad orchestrate?
faqs.answer: Containers, binaries, and batch jobs across cloud and on-premises infrastructure.

faqs.question: Does Nomad integrate with other HashiCorp tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Consul for service discovery and Vault for secrets management.

faqs.question: What does the Enterprise edition add?
faqs.answer: Additional access controls and governance policies, per HashiCorp's documentation.

use_cases.title: Hybrid workload orchestration
use_cases.description: Schedule containers and non-containerized apps across on-prem and cloud environments from one tool.

use_cases.title: Batch job scheduling
use_cases.description: Run batch and binary workloads without requiring full containerization.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Nomad?
paragraphs: Nomad is HashiCorp's scheduler and orchestrator for deploying and managing containers, binaries, and batch jobs across on-prem and cloud environments at scale.

It integrates with Consul for service discovery and Vault for secrets management, and is available as an open-source/Community edition alongside a paid Enterprise edition with added governance features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Nomad is for
paragraphs: Nomad suits infrastructure teams that need to orchestrate a mix of containerized and non-containerized workloads across hybrid on-prem and cloud environments.

enrichment.short_description: Consul is HashiCorp's service networking platform providing service discovery, service mesh, and secure service-to-service communication.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HashiCorp Consul: service discovery, service mesh, and secure network connectivity across on-prem, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments.

features.title: Service discovery
features.description: Service registration and discovery through a central catalog.

features.title: Service mesh
features.description: Sidecar proxies and gateways implement a full service mesh.

features.title: L7 traffic management
features.description: Application-layer traffic control across services.

features.title: Security enforcement
features.description: Intentions and access controls govern which services can communicate.

features.title: Observability
features.description: Metrics, logs, and distributed tracing for service traffic.

features.title: Multi-tenancy
features.description: Admin partitions and namespaces isolate tenants.

pros: Works across on-prem, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments
Supports both VM and Kubernetes runtimes
Built-in service mesh and traffic management
Managed HCP Consul option available
cons: Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed (quote-based)
Service mesh adds operational complexity
Full feature set is split across Community and Enterprise editions

faqs.question: Is Consul free?
faqs.answer: Consul has a Community edition; Enterprise and a managed HCP Consul option are also available.

faqs.question: What does Consul do?
faqs.answer: Provides service discovery, service mesh, and secure network connectivity between services.

faqs.question: Does Consul work with Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports both virtual machine and Kubernetes runtimes.

faqs.question: What is HCP Consul?
faqs.answer: HashiCorp's managed cloud version of Consul.

use_cases.title: Multi-cloud service networking
use_cases.description: Connect services securely across on-prem, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes service mesh
use_cases.description: Add service mesh traffic management and security to Kubernetes workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is HashiCorp Consul?
paragraphs: Consul is a service networking solution that enables teams to manage secure network connectivity between services across on-prem, hybrid cloud, and multi-cloud environments and runtimes.

It combines service discovery, a service mesh with sidecar proxies and gateways, L7 traffic management, and security enforcement through intentions and access controls, and is available as Community, Enterprise, and managed HCP Consul editions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Consul is for
paragraphs: Consul is built for platform and network teams that need service discovery and secure service-to-service communication across VM and Kubernetes environments spanning multiple clouds.

enrichment.short_description: Boundary is HashiCorp's identity-based access management tool for securely accessing infrastructure without exposing credentials or networks.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HashiCorp Boundary: identity-based, secure remote access to infrastructure without exposing networks or sharing credentials.

features.title: Identity-based access
features.description: Enable privileged sessions for users and applications based on identity and role.

features.title: Automated service discovery
features.description: Automates onboarding of infrastructure resources as hosts.

features.title: Terraform provider support
features.description: Define access policies and configuration as code with the full Terraform provider.

features.title: Session auditing
features.description: Visibility into session metrics, events, logs, and traces, with export to monitoring tools.

features.title: SSH credential injection
features.description: Injects SSH credentials for sessions instead of exposing them to end users.

pros: Identity-based access removes the need for VPNs or shared credentials
Integrates with Terraform for infrastructure-as-code policy management
Session auditing with export to monitoring tools
Both self-managed Community and managed HCP Boundary options available
cons: HCP Boundary pricing beyond the free trial is not published on the site
Requires integration work with existing identity providers
Fewer built-in integrations than more established PAM tools

faqs.question: Is Boundary free?
faqs.answer: A self-managed Community edition is available; HCP Boundary (managed) offers a free trial.

faqs.question: What problem does Boundary solve?
faqs.answer: Secure, identity-based remote access to infrastructure without exposing networks or sharing credentials.

faqs.question: Does Boundary integrate with Terraform?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has full Terraform provider support for policies and configuration.

faqs.question: What is HCP Boundary?
faqs.answer: HashiCorp's managed cloud offering for Boundary, available with a free trial.

use_cases.title: Privileged remote access
use_cases.description: Grant identity-based, time-limited access to servers and databases without VPNs.

use_cases.title: Session auditing and compliance
use_cases.description: Track and export session logs and metrics for audit and compliance needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Boundary?
paragraphs: Boundary is HashiCorp's identity-based access management tool that enables users and applications to securely access infrastructure based on identity and role, rather than network location.

It offers automated service discovery, Terraform provider support for policy as code, and session auditing, and is available as a self-managed Community edition or as managed HCP Boundary with a free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Boundary is for
paragraphs: Boundary is aimed at security and infrastructure teams that need to grant privileged, auditable access to servers and systems without relying on VPNs or shared credentials.

enrichment.short_description: Authelia is an open-source authentication and authorization server providing SSO and multi-factor authentication for self-hosted apps via a reverse proxy.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Authelia: free, open-source SSO and multi-factor authentication server that pairs with a reverse proxy to secure self-hosted apps.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication and SSO
features.description: Centralized authentication portal supporting single sign-on across web applications.

features.title: Modern authentication methods
features.description: Supports Passkeys, one-time passwords, mobile push notifications, and WebAuthn.

features.title: Login regulation
features.description: Brute-force prevention through login rate regulation.

features.title: Granular authorization policies
features.description: Fine-grained access control policies for users and groups.

features.title: OpenID Connect 1.0 provider
features.description: Operates as an OpenID Certified provider for standards-based SSO.

features.title: Lightweight footprint
features.description: Container image under 20MB with typical memory usage under 30MB.

features.title: Kubernetes support
features.description: Deployable for high availability on Kubernetes.

pros: Free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license
Very lightweight (container image under 20MB)
Fast performance, with ~100ms portal load and millisecond-level authorization
OpenID Certified provider
Supports modern auth methods like Passkeys and WebAuthn
cons: Requires a reverse proxy to function; not a standalone application
Self-hosted setup and configuration required
Relies on community contributions/donations to fund continued security audits

faqs.question: Is Authelia free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: What authentication methods does Authelia support?
faqs.answer: Passkeys, one-time passwords, mobile push notifications, and WebAuthn.

faqs.question: Does Authelia work standalone?
faqs.answer: No, it is designed as a companion to a reverse proxy.

faqs.question: Is Authelia OpenID certified?
faqs.answer: Yes, it operates as an OpenID Certified provider.

faqs.question: How lightweight is Authelia?
faqs.answer: The container image is under 20MB and typically uses under 30MB of memory.

use_cases.title: Securing self-hosted apps
use_cases.description: Add SSO and MFA in front of self-hosted web applications via a reverse proxy.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-style access control
use_cases.description: Apply granular, group-based authorization policies to internal web apps.
use_cases.audience: Small teams and organizations running internal tools

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Authelia?
paragraphs: Authelia is an open-source authentication and authorization server and portal that adds multi-factor authentication and single sign-on to web applications through a companion reverse proxy.
It supports Passkeys, one-time passwords, mobile push, and WebAuthn, operates as an OpenID Certified provider, and is designed to be lightweight, with a container image under 20MB.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Authelia is for
paragraphs: Authelia is built for self-hosters and teams who run their own web applications behind a reverse proxy and want centralized SSO and MFA without relying on a third-party identity provider.
